fact: A week-long preliminary bombardment preceded the infantry assault, with many shells failing to explode (duds) and failing to cut the German wire
source: The First Day on the Somme, Martin Middlebrook (1971)

fact: German dugouts were approximately thirty feet deep, protecting soldiers from the British bombardment
source: The First Day on the Somme, Martin Middlebrook (1971); Imperial War Museum

fact: British soldiers were ordered to advance at walking pace in lines across no man's land
source: The First Day on the Somme, Martin Middlebrook (1971)

fact: Approximately 19,240 British soldiers were killed on the first day (1 July 1916), commonly rounded to 'twenty thousand'
source: British Official History: Military Operations France and Belgium 1916, Vol. 1, James Edmonds (1932); widely documented

fact: The preliminary bombardment lasted seven days (24 June – 1 July 1916) and fired around 1.7 million shells
source: Imperial War Museum / National Army Museum; widely documented
